transcript.text: I didn't start reading seriously until
my early 30s. Now over a decade later, I
have read three 400 books and I've ran
into a few of them that I feel like,
damn, I wish I would have read these
earlier. Not because they made me
smarter, but because they would change
the way I moved through the world while
I was still trying to figure things out.
Your 20s is when you are figuring out
your foundations, your habits, your
world view, your relationship with work,
uh meaning and other people. These eight
books, they address exactly those
things. And all eight of these books are
books that I rate five out of five or
very close to that.
Every 20some asks, what's the meaning of
it all? And this book, Man's Search for
Meaning, has the answer. Victor Frankle,
he survived devastating
Nazi concentration camps and came out on
the other side with a simple devastating
insight. Meaning isn't something you
find. It's something that you choose.
Even in the darkest circumstances
imaginable. Purpose is available to
anyone who's willing to look for it. If
you're grappling with the big questions
for the first time, career, identity,
why any of it matters, then Victor
Frankle, he offers no platitude. He
offers proof that meaning can exist
anywhere even in suffering. This is a
book that crushes nihilism at its root.
Great book. Uh I've read it several
times. Flow. This is a great book
because a lot of people are looking for
happiness in the wrong places. Mien
talks about it's more important of how
you do something than what you do.
Engagement is the most important thing.
Mi Shinsen Mi he studied what make think
people enjoy what they do and he found
out that the most important ingredients
is how you engage with an activity. Flow
is a state of complete involvement. No
ego, total focus, time disappearing and
it can be cultivated in any activity. An
insight that stuck with me is turn
everything you do into a game. It's easy
for me to say because I'm a game
developer, but it makes sense. There are
certain things that makes video games
addictive and you can apply them into
your life. Clear objectives, immediate
feedback, clear challenges. These are
the same thing that makes life
satisfying. Think of life as a vast RPG
and you're leveling up your character.
This book will guide you how to do it.
Flow,
the psychology of optimal experience.
Uh, this is a great book that I wish
that I read earlier. The denial of death
by Ernest Becker. Most people don't
confront their mortality until they hit
their midlife crisis. Getting there
early changes everything. We all know
that we are going to die. This is the
unique problem of the conscious animal.
We know it, but we don't feel it because
we need to repress that truth in order
to function in normal life. So, what do
we do? We build what uh Becker calls the
vital lie. Something that transcends us.
Some system of ideas that makes that
terror manageable. A flag, a cause, a
career, a religion. Bicker's insight is
that we are gods with anuses. We have
these godlike minds that can imagine
almost anything that is boundless. And
then we have these animalistic
and very limited bodies. This paradox
drives human behavior in all sorts of
ways from our need for heroism to our
fear of being ordinary. I started this
book as one person and came out on the
other side as another person. It takes
you through Kirk Godard Freud young
masslov from each chapter of this book
demanded a period of reflection
afterwards. And here's the thing your
20-year-old self needs to hear. Anxiety
is the possibility of freedom. A
challenging book, but an important book
nonetheless.
Ancient philosophy that made me
unbreakable. This book is a guide to the
good life, the ancient art of stoic joy
by William B. Irvine. This is the most
accessible onramp for stoicism. And in
your 20ies is when you need it the most.
That's when life is full of drama,
career stress, breakups, social
comparison, financial anxiety. Stoicism
is the antidote. And this book is the
best way in. Irvin doesn't throw you in
the deep end and he translates the
ancient stoics uh the senicas and the
marcus Aurelius of the world into very
practical uh things that you can use in
modern life. You will learn about
negative visualization, the dichotomy of
control, how to deal with setbacks,
insults and desire. These aren't
abstract philosophical exercises. These
are tools that you can start to use
today. Once the stoic framework clicks,
everything becomes easier. Rejections,
bad bosses, failures,
uncertainty, all of it. A Guide to the
Good Life by William B. Irvine is a must
readad and one of my favorite books of
all time, and I wish I read it earlier.
Turning Pro by Steven Presfield is a
kick in the ass to stop dabbling and
start committing. Uh Presfield, he makes
a sharp line between the amateur and the
professional. The amateur is addicted to
comfort while the amateur shows up every
day, does the work, and don't wait for
inspiration. Inspiration won't be there
every time you need to get things done.
And you need to be ready to just sit
down and do the work. And this book
really got me into this mindset. I mean,
sometimes I don't want to make videos
and book recommendations. I rather sit
back on the balcony and have a pin
colada. But I'm committed to this and I
do this every week regardless of how I
feel. And that's what I learned from
this book and it's been a really really
good lesson. That creativity is actually
more about routine and commitment than
having the muses being on your side that
specific day when you need to do
something. You can read this book in an
afternoon, but you will feel it for
years. This is one of the most important
and impactful books that I've read. It
has helped me immensely in my life.
Turning Pro by Steven Presfield. Your
habits in your 20s literally builds the
person you become in your 30s. Atomic
Habits by James Clear is quite a
remarkable book. It shows how the
compound effect of tiny changes is the
most powerful force in personal
development and in your 20s is when the
runway is longest. Clear's framework is
simple and elegant. Make your good
habits accessible, attractive, easy to
do, and satisfying. Do the opposite to
your bad habits and design your
environment so that it's easy to do the
right thing and hard to do the bad
behavior. Have the candy unaccessible at
the top of this big drawer and have the
good snacks right at hand. The compound
effect is real tiny changes each day
makes for a compounding effects that
make you not being able to recognize
yourself in a couple of months and
years. Atomic Habits is the best habit
book on the market. It has stood the
test of time. Uh this is the one book
you have to read and implement. Quote,
"You don't rise to the levels of your
goals, you rise to the levels of your
systems. Read this and build good
systems in your life." Now, to the book
that I almost skipped, but that actually
changed how I think about money and
happiness. When I first heard about this
book on social media, I thought it would
be another mediocre book of some CEO who
just wanted to boost their ego. But in
fact, this is just a treasure trove of
wisdom and insights. Nava's concept of
specific knowledge changed how I think
about career building. What feels like
play to you but feels like work to
others? Answer that question and that's
your edge. For me, it was game
development and books. For you, it might
be something entirely different. The
point is to stop competing and start
being authentically undeniably yourself.
This book just has so much when it comes
to how to build relationship, how to
build wealth, how to be a lifelong
learner, how to learn anything. It's
it's a really good book. Uh I'm going to
read it several times and I keep coming
back to the concept of these books all
the time. It's a small investment of
your time to read it and the payoff is
just great. We're at the last book and
don't zone out yet because this one is
really important. It's influenced by
Professor Robert B. Shaldini, the
psychology of persuasion. I always say
this book should be mandatory in schools
because this book shows you how people
influence you and how you can influence
people. Every day you're bombarded with
people trying to influence you. If you
learn about the cognitive biases, the
heretics, the shortcuts your brain uh
takes and how they can be exploited, you
can actually protect yourself because
you can identify people trying to
manipulate you. This book is five out of
five book. It's mandatory reading. It's
something you need to fully understand
in order to comprehend and understand
the world fully. It's also an
entertaining book. It's fun to read. The
examples are amazing. If you don't know
all your cognitive biases, read this
book. This book is going to help you for
the rest of your life. I wish I read it
earlier. You have the chance to do it.
So, that was the list. I hope you
enjoyed it. This is a bit of a haphazard
video. A lot of different
I recorded this in several chunks, so
it's a bit here and there. Hopefully,
you got something out of it. Next week
will be the breakdown of the upcoming
non-fiction releases. I will also have
my newsletter coming up where you can
get I don't know if it's going to be
monthly or weekly yet, but sign up. It
will be very short and it will be
concise and it will be full of book
recommendations. Uh so that's something
you can get in your inbox if you want to
have that constant flow of new reading
inspiration. Anyway, thanks for tuning
in and happy reading. See you soon. Be
out.
